hard_spots.problem: The `prefix=f"{prefix}.<name>"` mechanism is mentioned in passing but its importance (linking module hierarchy to checkpoint weight names) isn't explained until §26.7. Readers don't initially know why they should track this parameter chain.

The text suddenly introduces `make_layers()` as the "standard vLLM signal" for stacked layers without first explaining what it does or why it's syntactically different from regular `self.X = Module()`. The `× N` notation appears without prior definition.
`nn.ModuleList` is mentioned as equivalent to `make_layers` but never explained. Readers unfamiliar with PyTorch don't know what this class does or how it differs from a nested `nn.Module`.
The distinction between `nn.Module` submodules vs `nn.Parameter`/buffer is asserted without explaining the syntactic signal readers should look for to distinguish them when scanning `__init__` code.

Shape changes are explained using only symbolic notation (`[T,H]→[T,hc_mult,H]`). No concrete numeric example (e.g., `[1024, 4096] → [1024, 4, 4096]` with explicit values) makes the mental model harder to build.
`_mtp_hidden_buffer` is discussed as a branch/data endpoint without explaining where it was defined, its purpose, or why it appears here. Assumes readers retained ch25's MTP concept.

Three judgment criteria (torch.ops.*, @torch.compile, parallel class names) are presented sequentially in code blocks, but their relationship to the overarching concept of "subsystem coloring" is unclear. These feel like three separate signals presented as one.

The visual convention for "optional frames" and "mutually exclusive branches" on a diagram is described in words only. Readers don't see what these actually look like, making it impossible to reproduce the pattern on other models.
Aux CUDA streams are mentioned as a signal for parallel/communication subsystems, but the text never explains why aux streams specifically indicate this or what the 3 streams are doing in practice.

The three verification criteria are listed, but the actual process of "tracing back" is not mechanized. Readers don't know whether to compare line-by-line, write a linter, or use some other verification method.

diagram_suggestions.what: Side-by-side diagram showing (LEFT) `self.X = Module()` code lines → ownership boxes; (RIGHT) `hidden_states = self.child()` code lines → directed edges; (BOTTOM) resulting architecture diagram with nested boxes and data flow arrows. Visually bridges the code-to-diagram mapping before diving into examples.

Three-panel comparison: (TOP) single module declaration `self.layer = Module()` → one box; (MIDDLE) manual repetition `self.layer_0/1/2...` → N boxes; (BOTTOM) `make_layers(N, lambda)` → one box with ×N label. Clarifies why collapse to one box is correct and why N separate boxes would be wrong.

Concrete shape trace: INPUT tensor `[1024, 4096]` (labeled T=tokens, H=hidden) → OPERATOR `repeat(1, 4, 1)` with hc_mult=4 visualized → OUTPUT `[1024, 4, 4096]` with dimension growth shown. Show how to label this on a diagram edge (`[T,H]→[T,hc_mult,H]`).

Three-column diagram: (COLUMN 1) torch.ops.vllm code → orange box for self-defined operator; (COLUMN 2) @torch.compile decorator → purple dashed border for compiled region; (COLUMN 3) ColumnParallelLinear class name → colored box for parallel type. Shows all three signals in visual parallel, not sequential text.

Two architecture diagrams side-by-side: (LEFT) with `config.n_shared_experts is None` showing shared_experts box grayed or dotted (conditional); (RIGHT) without it when condition is false. Shows visual convention for conditional frames so readers can recognize pattern in future models.

notes: This chapter teaches a four-step mechanical procedure (P1→P2→P3→P4) for converting model code to architecture diagrams—a solid methodology. However, implementation suffers from: (1) forward references that aren't resolved until later sections (e.g., `prefix` importance), (2) abrupt introduction of key concepts like `make_layers()` without prior definition, (3) sequential presentation of orthogonal judgment criteria (torch.ops, @torch.compile, parallel class names) instead of side-by-side comparison, (4) visual conventions described only in words, never shown, making patterns hard for readers to reproduce independently. The chapter assumes readers have already deeply internalized ch25's DeepSeek-V4 source and PyTorch `nn.Module` design. Adding 5 targeted diagrams and a few brief clarifications of PyTorch basics would dramatically improve followability."
